does: This paper introduces MENTIS, a geometry-first framework that measures internal computational changes in language models after preference alignment by comparing instruction-tuned and preference-aligned model checkpoints. The core method uses three complementary metrics: a layerwise covariance-based torsion norm (T1) measuring geometric distortion of hidden representations, a spectral torsion diagnostic (T2) for fine-grained analysis, and an Energy-Radiance-Activation measure (ERA) for localizing changes across model depth. Applied to four 7-8B model pairs on the LITMUS benchmark, MENTIS reveals that alignment produces selective rather than uniform internal reorganization, with different concepts and model families showing varying degrees of geometric restructuring in their latent spaces.
connects: This work builds on prior research examining the internal mechanisms of language model alignment and the geometry of neural representations. It extends earlier investigations into how post-training reshapes model behavior by providing concrete geometric tools for quantifying these changes, moving beyond behavior-level evaluation that has dominated alignment research. The approach connects to broader work on neural representation geometry, mechanistic interpretability, and the persistent failures of aligned systems under adversarial conditions like jailbreaks and prompt injection. By focusing on layerwise geometric structure rather than individual neurons or attention heads, MENTIS complements prior mechanistic studies while responding to the observation that behavioral alignment leaves vulnerabilities suggesting incomplete internal reorganization.
enables: This framework enables deeper investigation into why aligned models remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks despite improved surface behavior, potentially revealing which internal structures require stronger alignment constraints. The selective reorganization patterns MENTIS identifies could inform more targeted alignment methods that focus on problematic geometric regions rather than uniform optimization. Future work could use these geometric insights to design alignment techniques that produce more robust internal structures, predict failure modes from latent geometry, or understand why different model families exhibit varying alignment signatures. The framework also opens directions for analyzing how alignment affects different concept families differently, potentially enabling more nuanced alignment strategies that preserve beneficial capabilities while constraining harmful behaviors.
